text: Hastelloy N composed of $7\%$ Cr, $16\%$ Mo, balance nickel, was used to contain the $\mathrm{LiF - BeF_2 - ZrF_4 - UF_4}$ fuel salt in the Molten Salt Reactor Experiment (MSRE).

Surface chemical analyses of the affected grain boundaries indicated the presence of the fission product tellurium. $^{1,2}$ Because the depth of cracking observed in the MSRE would not be acceptable when extrapolated to the 30-year design life of a Molten-Salt Breeder Reactor (MSBR), work began to provide for the MSBR primary containment vessel a material that would resist grain boundary attack by tellurium.
Our first tellurium experiment (pot 1) was in the form of a static pot built to evaluate the use of lithium telluride as a means for adding tellurium to salt.
1) allows tellurium to be added periodically in the form of salt pellets containing a measured amount of lithium telluride.
About $600\mathrm{ml}$ of the salt $\mathrm{LiF - BeF_2 - ThF_4}$ (72-16-12 mol %) was used, and its temperature was maintained at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
Initially, two pellets, containing a total of $70\mathrm{mg}$ of $\mathrm{Li_2Te}$ , were added to $600\mathrm{ml}$ of salt.
Three additions of $\mathrm{CrF}_2$ totalling $1.82\mathrm{g}$ were then made, followed by the addition of three more $\mathrm{Li}_2\mathrm{Te}$ pellets.
A final addition consisting of $0.2\mathrm{g}$ of BeO was made.
Chemical analysis of the salt sample taken after the addition of five $\mathrm{Li}_2\mathrm{Te}$ pellets showed that the tellurium content was less than 5 ppm.
A second experiment was conducted in the same static pot at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ using the reportedly more soluble lithium telluride, $\mathrm{LiTe}_3$ .
After the pot had been drained and cleaned, fresh salt was added, and three pellets containing a total of about $0.1\mathrm{g}$ of $\mathrm{LiTe}_3$ were inserted.
After about $650\mathrm{hr}$ the specimen was removed from the salt, tensile-tested to failure, and examined metallographically.

A Hastelloy N pot was filled with about $500\mathrm{ml}$ of the salt $\mathrm{LiF - BeF_2 - }$ $\mathrm{ThF_4}$ (72-16-12 mol %), and the temperature of the pot was controlled at $700^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
After 170 hr the specimen was removed and after 250 hr a salt sample was taken.
The temperature was then lowered to $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , and a day later another salt sample was taken.
This sequence was then repeated at $600^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
Next, the salt temperature was raised to $700^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , $\mathrm{Cr_2Te_3}$ was added, and another Hastelloy N specimen inserted.

Tellurium concentrations at $700^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ were not as high as was expected, but some tellurium was in solution as demonstrated by the tellurium found on the grain boundaries of the specimens.
Both specimens, one of standard Hastelloy N and one of $2.6\%$ Nb-0.7% Ti-modified Hastelloy N, showed a weight increase after 500 hr exposure at $700^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .

Tensile specimens of several modified Hastelloy N alloys with different combinations of niobium, titanium, and chromium were exposed to the salt- $\mathrm{Cr_3Te_4}$ system at $700^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
Examination of these specimens after tensile testing gave results included in Table 2.
As part of the Auger examination of these specimens, the depth of tellurium penetration was measured as a function of exposure time.<sup>3</sup> These results are shown in Fig.
For grain boundary diffusion where the diffusing species is soluble in the bulk, a $1/4$ power dependence is predicted.
If essentially no bulk solubility is assumed, a power dependence of $1/2$ is expected.
4, these data fit a $t^{1/2}$ plot reasonably well.

Brynestad<sup>4</sup> reported that a Hastelloy N specimen exposed to vapor above $\mathrm{Ni}_{3}\mathrm{Te}_{2} + \mathrm{Ni}$ for 1000 hr at $700^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ did not show intergranular cracking.
This suggested that the $\mathrm{Ni}_{3}\mathrm{Te}_{2} + \mathrm{Ni}$ system has a tellurium activity at which Hastelloy N is not attacked.
To further evaluate this potentially significant result, a test was designed to expose Hastelloy N tensile specimens to salt containing $\mathrm{Ni}_{3}\mathrm{Te}_{2} + \mathrm{Ni}$ .
A capsule was built and filled with about 310 ml of LiF-BeF<sub>2</sub>-ThF<sub>4</sub> salt.

A mixture of 62 at. % Ni-38 at. % Te, which had been annealed at high temperature to promote formation of $\mathrm{Ni}_{3}\mathrm{Te}_{2}$ plus a small excess of nickel, was added to the capsule, and four Hastelloy N specimens were inserted.
One specimen was removed after 1000 hr at $700^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , strained to failure, and examined
All three remaining specimens, which were exposed for longer times at $700^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , showed intergranular attack, and the results of metallographic examinations are given in Table 3.

Because other experiments were indicating that alloying with 1 to $2\%$ Nb lessens the tellurium embrittlement of Hastelloy N, four additional modified specimens were exposed to this $\mathrm{Ni}_3\mathrm{Te}_2$ -salt mixture in the capsule described above.
The specimens contained additions of 0, 1.1, 2.0, and $4.4\%$ Nb and all were exposed for about 3000 hr at $700^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
Results are included in Table 3.

Up to 100 specimens can be exposed to salt at one time (Figs.
The pot was filled with about 11 liters of $\mathrm{LiF - BeF_2 - ThF_4}$ salt, and a mixture of $\mathrm{Cr_3Te_4}$ and $\mathrm{Cr_5Te_6}$ was added.

Specimens that had been selected from the more than 50 different available modified and standard Hastelloy N compositions were exposed to this salt-telluride mixture for times ranging from 250 to 5000 hr at $700^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
The most notable result of these tests was the observation that the addition of 1 to 2 at. % Nb to Hastelloy N significantly reduced the cracking, as shown in Figs.

It has been suggested<sup>7</sup> that since chromium ion activity can be controlled, within a certain range, by the U(IV)/U(III) ratio, it might be possible to control tellurium activity by "complexing" reactions with chromium or other salt constituents, for example,
Electrochemical studies reported by Manning and Mamantov<sup>8</sup> indicated that in a relatively reducing $\mathrm{LiF - BeF_2 - ThF_4}$ melt, tellurium could exist

Their calculations showed the critical potential for the formation of a telluride corresponded to a U(IV)/U(III) ratio of about 150 at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
To check this hypothesis, a salt pot was built, equipped with electrochemical probes, and filled with about 1.4 liters of MSBR fuel salt $(\mathrm{LiF - BeF_2 - ThF_4 - UF_4})$ .
Additions of $\mathrm{CrFe_2}$ and about $1\mathrm{g}$ of $\mathrm{Cr_3Te_4}$ were made, and a Hastelloy N specimen was exposed for 500 hr to salt with a U(IV)/U(III) ratio of about 90.
After another addition of $\mathrm{CrF_2}$ and $\mathrm{Cr_3Te_4}$ to the salt, another specimen was exposed to the mixture for 500 hr.
10) of the tensile tested specimen showed a complete absence of grain boundary cracks.
Specimens were exposed for about 260 hr at U(IV)/U(III) ratios of approximately 10, 30, 60, 85, and 300.
12, a marked change occurred in the cracking behavior of Hastelloy N exposed in this system in going from a ratio of about 60 to about 100.

(Also different was the telluride added, $\mathrm{CrTe}_{1.333}$ vs $\mathrm{CrTe}_{1.266}$ , but from previous experience we would not expect it to make such a large difference).
The addition of 1 to 2 at. % Nb significantly reduces embrittlement, but small additions of titanium or additions of up to 15 at. % Cr do not affect embrittlement.
